blocks.title: Knee RTP - Tier 0: Early Protection
blocks.description: Immediate post-surgical or acute injury phase. Focus on pain/swelling management, ROM restoration, and quad activation.
entry_criteria: Post-op day 0-14 or acute injury <2 weeks
No signs of infection or DVT
exit_criteria: Quad set with visible VMO contraction

exercises.name: Quad Sets
exercises.notes: VMO activation emphasis, towel under knee

evidence: Shelbourne KD, et al. Accelerated rehabilitation after anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction. Am J Sports Med. 1990;18(3):292-299.

van Melick N, et al. Evidence-based clinical practice update: practice guidelines for anterior cruciate ligament rehabilitation based on a systematic review and multidisciplinary consensus. Br J Sports Med. 2016;50(24):1506-1515.
precautions: Monitor for signs of infection (fever, redness, warmth)
Avoid forced ROM - let pain guide
Elevate leg frequently to control swelling
progressions: Increase ROM target by 10° per week
Add resistance once SLR without lag achieved

blocks.title: Knee RTP - Tier 1: ROM & Strength Foundation
blocks.description: Restore full ROM, develop basic strength, normalize gait. Weeks 2-6 post-op or equivalent injury stage.
exit_criteria: Normal gait pattern without assistive device

exercises.name: Mini Squats (0-60°)
exercises.notes: Focus on knee tracking over toes

evidence: Adams D, et al. Current concepts for anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction. Am J Sports Med. 2012;40(10):2343-2356.
Myer GD, et al. Rehabilitation after anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction: criteria-based progression through the return-to-sport phase. J Orthop Sports Phys Ther. 2006;36(6):385-402.
precautions: Monitor for swelling after each session
progressions: Increase load 5-10% when reps achieved pain-free

blocks.title: Knee RTP - Tier 2: Strength & Neuromuscular Control
blocks.description: Develop strength >80% of uninvolved limb, introduce controlled dynamic movements. Weeks 6-12 post-op.
exit_criteria: Single-leg squat depth >60° with good form

exercises.name: Lateral Step-Downs
exercises.notes: Eccentric quad control, knee valgus prevention

evidence: Hewett TE, et al. Biomechanical measures of neuromuscular control and valgus loading of the knee predict anterior cruciate ligament injury risk in female athletes. Am J Sports Med. 2005;33(4):492-501.

Paterno MV, et al. Biomechanical measures during landing and postural stability predict second anterior cruciate ligament injury after reconstruction and return to sport. Am J Sports Med. 2010;38(10):1968-1978.
precautions: Monitor for knee valgus during single-leg tasks

blocks.title: Knee RTP - Tier 3: Power & Plyometric Introduction
blocks.description: Introduce jumping, landing mechanics, and power development. Weeks 12-16 post-op.
entry_criteria: No pain or swelling with Tier 2 exercises
exit_criteria: Single-leg hop test >90% LSI (Limb Symmetry Index)
Drop vertical jump with good landing mechanics
No pain or swelling with plyometrics

exercises.name: Double-Leg Box Jumps (Low Height)
exercises.notes: 12-18 inch box, focus on landing mechanics

exercises.name: Deceleration Training (Drop and Hold)
exercises.notes: Step off 12-18 inch box, absorb and freeze

evidence: Myer GD, et al. The effects of plyometric versus dynamic stabilization and balance training on lower extremity biomechanics. Am J Sports Med. 2006;34(3):445-455.
Paterno MV, et al. Incidence of second ACL injuries 2 years after primary reconstruction and return to sport. Am J Sports Med. 2014;42(7):1567-1573.
precautions: Start with double-leg before single-leg plyometrics
Ice after sessions if any swelling
progressions: Increase box height 2-4 inches as tolerated

blocks.title: Knee RTP - Tier 4: Advanced Plyometrics & Agility
blocks.description: High-level plyometrics, change of direction, sport-specific movements. Weeks 16-20 post-op.
exit_criteria: No pain, swelling, or apprehension with cutting
evidence: Kyritsis P, et al. Likelihood of ACL graft rupture: not meeting six clinical discharge criteria before return to sport is associated with a four times greater risk of rupture. Br J Sports Med. 2016;50(15):946-951.

Webster KE, et al. What is the evidence for and validity of return-to-sport testing after anterior cruciate ligament reconstruction surgery? A systematic review and meta-analysis. Sports Med. 2019;49(6):917-929.
precautions: Gradually increase cutting speed and angles
progressions: Progress from controlled to reactive drills
Increase volume and intensity toward sport demands

blocks.title: Knee RTP - Tier 5: Return to Sport Integration
blocks.description: Full sport integration, match demands, psychological readiness. Weeks 20+ post-op (typically 9-12 months).
exit_criteria: Full participation in sport without restriction
maintenance_exercises: Monitor for fatigue and adjust volume
evidence: Grindem H, et al. Simple decision rules can reduce reinjury risk by 84% after ACL reconstruction. Br J Sports Med. 2016;50(13):804-808.
Ardern CL, et al. Psychological aspects of anterior cruciate ligament injuries. Oper Tech Sports Med. 2016;24(1):77-83.
precautions: Gradual return - no full games immediately
Monitor for late-stage swelling or fatigue
progressions: Return to competition when all criteria met

blocks.title: Shoulder RTP - Tier 0: Early Protection
blocks.description: Immediate post-surgical phase or acute injury. Focus on protection, pain control, and passive ROM.
entry_criteria: Post-op day 0-14 or acute injury <2 weeks
exit_criteria: Passive ROM: forward flexion >90°, ER >20° at side

exercises.name: Pendulum Exercises
exercises.notes: Relax shoulder, allow gravity to mobilize

evidence: Wilk KE, et al. Rehabilitation of the overhead throwing athlete. J Orthop Sports Phys Ther. 1993;18(2):433-441.
Ellenbecker TS, et al. Rehabilitation of shoulder impingement syndrome and rotator cuff injuries. Clin Sports Med. 2003;22(4):703-733.
precautions: Respect post-surgical precautions (e.g., no ER >30° for labral repair)
No active ROM yet if contraindicated
Monitor for increased pain or swelling
progressions: Increase ROM target by 10° per week

blocks.title: Shoulder RTP - Tier 1: ROM & Isometric Strength
blocks.description: Restore ROM, initiate strengthening with isometrics, scapular stabilization. Weeks 2-6 post-op.

exercises.name: Active-Assisted ROM (All Planes)
exercises.notes: Flexion, abduction, ER, IR - full available ROM

evidence: Ellenbecker TS, et al. Rehabilitation of shoulder impingement syndrome and rotator cuff injuries. Clin Sports Med. 2003;22(4):703-733.
Reinold MM, et al. Current concepts in the evaluation and treatment of the shoulder in overhead throwing athletes, part 2. Sports Health. 2010;2(2):101-115.
precautions: Respect surgical precautions for ROM limits
Maintain proper scapular position during all exercises
progressions: Progress from isometric to isotonic when pain-free

blocks.title: Shoulder RTP - Tier 2: Strengthening & Neuromuscular Control
blocks.description: Develop rotator cuff and scapular strength, normalize shoulder biomechanics. Weeks 6-12 post-op.

exercises.name: Rhythmic Stabilization Drills
exercises.load: Manual resistance or ball on wall

evidence: Kibler WB, et al. Clinical implications of scapular dyskinesis in shoulder injury. Br J Sports Med. 2012;46(8):542-547.
Wilk KE, et al. Current concepts: the stabilizing structures of the glenohumeral joint. J Orthop Sports Phys Ther. 1997;25(6):364-379.
precautions: Monitor for shoulder impingement during overhead activities
Maintain scapular stability during all exercises
Avoid heavy loads - focus on form and control
progressions: Increase load 1-2 lb when reps achieved

blocks.title: Shoulder RTP - Tier 3: Advanced Strengthening & Power
blocks.description: Develop strength >80% uninvolved, introduce plyometrics, begin throwing prep. Weeks 12-16 post-op.
evidence: Reinold MM, et al. Effect of a 6-week weighted baseball throwing program on pitch velocity, pitching arm biomechanics, and arm strength. Sports Health. 2018;10(4):327-333.
Wilk KE, et al. Correlation of glenohumeral internal rotation deficit and total rotational motion to shoulder injuries in professional baseball pitchers. Am J Sports Med. 2011;39(2):329-335.
precautions: Monitor for GIRD (glenohumeral internal rotation deficit)
Assess for scapular dyskinesia during dynamic tasks
progressions: Increase plyometric ball weight and intensity
Progress to throwing program (if overhead athlete)

blocks.title: Shoulder RTP - Tier 4: Throwing Program & Sport Prep
blocks.description: Interval throwing program, sport-specific activities, advanced power. Weeks 16-20 post-op (overhead athletes).
exit_criteria: No pain or fatigue with throwing
evidence: Axe MJ, et al. The effect of a training program on the progression of throwing velocity in youth baseball pitchers. J Sports Med Phys Fitness. 2016;56(6):765-771.
Reinold MM, et al. Current concepts in the evaluation and treatment of the shoulder in overhead throwing athletes, part 2. Sports Health. 2010;2(2):101-115.
precautions: Follow structured throwing program - no skipping phases
progressions: Progress to bullpen work (pitchers) or position-specific drills

blocks.title: Shoulder RTP - Tier 5: Return to Sport
blocks.description: Full sport participation, competition readiness, performance optimization. Typically 6-9 months post-op.

exercises.name: Sport-Specific Throwing/Serving
exercises.notes: Full bullpen, batting practice, serving, etc.

evidence: Wilk KE, et al. Rehabilitation of the overhead throwing athlete. J Orthop Sports Phys Ther. 1993;18(2):433-441.
Manske RC, et al. Functional performance testing for power and return to sports. Sports Health. 2013;5(3):244-250.
precautions: Watch for GIRD or strength deficits
progressions: Progress from limited innings/sets to full workload
Optimize performance with continued strength work

blocks.title: Elbow RTP - Tier 0: Early Protection
blocks.description: Immediate post-surgical phase (e.g., UCL reconstruction). Focus on protection, ROM restoration, and grip strength.
evidence: Wilk KE, et al. Rehabilitation following elbow surgery in the throwing athlete. Oper Tech Sports Med. 1996;4(2):114-132.
Cain EL, et al. Outcome of ulnar collateral ligament reconstruction of the elbow in 1281 athletes. Am J Sports Med. 2010;38(12):2426-2434.
progressions: Increase ROM by 5-10° per week as cleared
Progress to active ROM when brace removed

blocks.title: Elbow RTP - Tier 1: Strengthening & Full ROM
blocks.description: Restore full ROM, develop elbow and forearm strength. Weeks 6-12 post-op (UCL reconstruction).
evidence: Ellenbecker TS, et al. Rehabilitation of the elbow following arthroscopy. Clin Sports Med. 2010;29(4):697-724.
Wilk KE, et al. Rehabilitation following elbow surgery in the throwing athlete. Oper Tech Sports Med. 1996;4(2):114-132.
precautions: Monitor for pain - adjust load accordingly
Ice after exercises if swelling present
progressions: Increase load 1-2 lb per week when reps achieved

blocks.title: Elbow RTP - Tier 2: Advanced Strengthening & Throwing Prep
blocks.description: Develop strength >80% uninvolved, initiate throwing program. Weeks 12-20 post-op (UCL reconstruction).
exit_criteria: No pain or swelling with throwing

exercises.name: Valgus Stress Drills (Protected)
exercises.notes: Gradually expose UCL to controlled stress

evidence: Reinold MM, et al. Current concepts in the evaluation and treatment of the shoulder in overhead throwing athletes, part 2. Sports Health. 2010;2(2):101-115.
Wilk KE, et al. Rehabilitation of the overhead throwing athlete. J Orthop Sports Phys Ther. 1993;18(2):433-441.
precautions: Follow throwing program strictly - no shortcuts
progressions: Advance throwing program per protocol phases
Progress to long toss when cleared

blocks.title: Elbow RTP - Tier 3: Return to Sport
blocks.description: Complete throwing progression, return to competition. Weeks 20+ post-op (typically 12+ months for UCL).
exit_criteria: Return to bullpen/mound (pitchers) or position play
evidence: Cain EL, et al. Outcome of ulnar collateral ligament reconstruction of the elbow in 1281 athletes. Am J Sports Med. 2010;38(12):2426-2434.
Wilk KE, et al. Rehabilitation following elbow surgery in the throwing athlete. Oper Tech Sports Med. 1996;4(2):114-132.
precautions: Monitor throwing volume carefully first season
progressions: Gradual return to competition (not full workload immediately)
Progress from non-save situations to high-leverage (pitchers)
Monitor performance and adjust volume as needed

blocks.title: Trunk/Core - Tier 0: Foundation
blocks.description: Basic core stability, anti-movement patterns. Foundation for all athletes.
exit_criteria: No low back pain with exercises
evidence: McGill SM, et al. Endurance times for low back stabilization exercises. Arch Phys Med Rehabil. 1999;80(8):941-944.
McGill SM. Low back stability: from formal description to issues for performance and rehabilitation. Exerc Sport Sci Rev. 2001;29(1):26-31.
precautions: Stop if low back pain increases
progressions: Increase hold times before adding external load

blocks.title: Trunk/Core - Tier 1: Anti-Rotation
blocks.description: Develop anti-rotation strength and dynamic stability. Essential for rotational athletes.
evidence: Hibbs AE, et al. Optimizing performance by improving core stability and core strength. Sports Med. 2008;38(12):995-1008.
Kibler WB, et al. The role of core stability in athletic function. Sports Med. 2006;36(3):189-198.
precautions: Maintain proper alignment during all exercises
No excessive spinal rotation under load initially

blocks.title: Trunk/Core - Tier 2: Dynamic Strength
blocks.description: Advanced core strength, loaded carries, dynamic stability. For high-level athletes.
exit_criteria: Farmer carry 1.5x BW per hand x 40m
evidence: McGill SM, et al. Endurance times for low back stabilization exercises. Arch Phys Med Rehabil. 1999;80(8):941-944.
Willardson JM. Core stability training: applications to sports conditioning programs. J Strength Cond Res. 2007;21(3):979-985.
precautions: Master form before increasing load significantly
No spinal flexion under heavy load
progressions: Increase carry load by 10-20 lb when distance maintained
Progress ab rollout from knees to feet

blocks.title: Trunk/Core - Tier 3: Power & Sport Integration
blocks.description: Explosive rotational power, sport-specific core demands. Elite athlete preparation.
exit_criteria: No core stability deficits under fatigue

exercises.name: Sport-Specific Core Integration
exercises.notes: Throwing, hitting, kicking with core emphasis

evidence: Szymanski DJ, et al. Effect of torso rotational strength on angular hip, angular shoulder, and linear bat velocities of high school baseball players. J Strength Cond Res. 2009;23(6):1681-1687.
Tse MA, et al. Effect of strength training on sport performance: a meta-analysis. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2005;37(10):1667-1673.
precautions: Ensure adequate warm-up before explosive work
progressions: Increase implement weight for power exercises
Optimize power output through velocity tracking
